chords.noVoicing: No voicing available for this combination
chords.howToReadDesc: The diagram is your guitar neck seen from the front, as if you're looking at someone else playing. Vertical lines are strings (thick on the left, thin on the right). Horizontal lines are frets. Put your fingers where the dots are.
chords.descX: Do not play this string. When strumming, skip it or mute it with a nearby finger
chords.descO: Play this string open — let it ring without pressing any fret
chords.descR: Root note — the main note the chord is named after. Shown in red
chords.descDot: Press this fret with your finger. Place your fingertip right behind the metal fret bar
chords.descBarre: Barre — lay one finger flat across several strings at this fret, pressing them all down at once
chords.descFr: Fret number — tells you where on the neck this shape is played. No number means the chord is at the beginning of the neck, near the tuning pegs

chordDesc.major: The "default" happy, bright chord. The most common type you'll learn first
chordDesc.minor: Sad, dark sounding. Written as Am, Em, Dm. Just one note lower than major
chordDesc.dom7: Adds a bluesy tension to a major chord. Common in blues, jazz, funk
chordDesc.min7: A softer, jazzy version of minor. Very common in R&B and neo-soul
chordDesc.maj7: Dreamy, lush major. Sounds sophisticated — think jazz and bossa nova
chordDesc.dim: Tense and unstable. Used as a passing chord between two other chords
chordDesc.aug: Mysterious, slightly unsettling. Rare but adds dramatic effect
chordDesc.sus2: Neither major nor minor — open and airy. Great for ambient and post-rock
chordDesc.sus4: Creates a "hanging" tension that wants to resolve back to major
chordDesc.power: Just two notes — root and 5th. Neither major nor minor. The backbone of rock and metal

cheat.whyC: Why C and not A? In medieval times the alphabet started from A — the lowest note of the system. Later, when major scale became dominant, C major (no sharps/flats) became the reference point. The letters stayed but the starting point shifted to C.

intRef.b3: Minor 3rd — 3 semitones, defines a minor key*
intRef.3: Major 3rd — 4 semitones, defines a major key*
intRef.b5: Tritone — 6 semitones, devil's interval, maximum tension
intRef.b7: Minor 7th — 10 semitones, bluesy, wants to resolve
intRef.footnote: * A common misconception is that minor means «sad» and major means «happy». In reality, the key is defined solely by the third: a minor (i.e. «smaller») third gives minor, a major (i.e. «larger») third gives major. The emotional character depends on tempo, rhythm, harmony, and context. Minor can sound powerful, aggressive, or triumphant, while major can sound melancholic, unsettling, or sorrowful.

character.dorian: Minor with a bright 6th degree. Jazz, funk, blues-rock.
character.phrygian: Dark with Spanish/Eastern flavor. Death metal, flamenco.
character.locrian: Darkest mode. No pure 5th — tritone instead. Unstable, anxious sound.
character.ionian: Standard major scale. Bright, happy sound.
character.aeolian: Standard minor scale. Sad, melancholic sound.
character.mixolydian: Major with flat 7th. Bluesy, rock character.
character.lydian: Major with raised 4th. Dreamy, floating sound.
character.harmonicMinor: Minor with raised 7th. Neoclassical, Eastern motifs.
character.melodicMinor: Minor with raised 6th and 7th. Jazz, fusion.
character.minorPenta: Blues and rock foundation. 5 notes, everything sounds good together.
character.majorPenta: Bright, open sound. Country, pop, rock.
